topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights hit the canvas, stark and unforgiving, illuminating my portrait—a piece I’d spent six months pouring my soul into. I stood near the entrance, a knot tightening in my stomach, watching people mill about. Then I saw it. My painting, hanging prominently on the main wall, bathed in an undeserved spotlight. A young woman with a bright, almost challenging expression leaned close to it, tracing the texture with a fingertip. My jaw tightened. I took a hesitant step forward, my polished shoes clicking loudly on the marble floor, intending to speak, but the words caught in my throat. A wave of coldness washed over me, making my palms sweat. Who had the right to display my vision like this?

[story 2]

I found myself in the quiet corner of the studio, staring at the framed photograph of my latest series. It was there, tucked awkwardly between a print of a cityscape and a stack of invoices, displayed on a temporary easel in the hallway of the coffee shop. My breath hitched. I walked over, my movements stiff, and peered at the work. It was mine. A sudden, sharp intake of air escaped me. I reached out, my fingers hovering just above the frame, trembling slightly. A murmur of conversation drifted from the nearby tables, oblivious to the silent tension radiating off me. I slowly retreated, my gaze fixed on the easel, a profound sense of disorientation settling over my chest.

[story 3]

The gallery opening was in full swing, a vibrant, chaotic symphony of chatter and clinking glasses. I was nursing a lukewarm drink, trying to appear composed, when I caught sight of my sculpture—the one I’d been agonizing over for months—situated near the entrance, inexplicably positioned next to someone else’s abstract piece. My eyes narrowed. I watched as a group of patrons clustered around it, pointing and nodding with an air of ownership. I felt a strange lightness, as if the ground beneath me had shifted. I gripped my glass so tightly my knuckles turned white, the noise of the room suddenly receding into a dull, distant hum. I needed to move, to reclaim what was mine, but my feet felt rooted to the spot.
